coredata.dc:title: Non-toxic fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel based on wood-derived cellulose nanocrystals and carbon dots for efficient sorption and sensitive detection of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Qiuyan
He, Shan
Huang, Yifeng
Lei, Zihua
Qiao, Jianzheng
Li, Qing
Xu, Dong
Guo, Xin
Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: For clean, effective, and high value-added use of wood, a novel non-toxic fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel (FMIH) was prepared based on wood-derived cellulose nanocrystals and carbon dots for efficient sorption and sensitive detection of tetracycline (TC). The FMIH’s morphology, chemical structure, optical properties and non-cytotoxicity were investigated. The adsorption performance of FMIH toward TC was studied, indicating that the sorption data were well fitted by the Langmuir and Pseudo-second-order models, and the maximum sorption capacity was 544.4 mg/g. An evaluation of the detection performance of FMIH toward TC showed its excellent selectivity and sensitivity to TC with the detection limit of 0.11 μg/L. Mechanism analysis confirmed that the efficient sorption and sensitive detection was due to specific molecular recognition sites introduced by wood-derived cellulose nanocrystals and carbon dots. Moreover, a cost analysis performed for FMIH and commercial activated carbon (CAC) showed that the cost of removing 1 g of TC using FMIH was almost thirty-four times cheaper than that of CAC. This work could provide a new way for using wood to produce a low-cost, multifunctional, and green adsorbent for removing antibiotics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Wood
Selective sorption
Antibiotics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM micrograph; (b) XRD patterns; (c) FTIR spectra; (d) XPS survey spectra; (e-f) XPS results of O1s and N1s; (g) Optical properties; (h) Emission spectra with varying excitation wavelengths; (i) Photo-stability during different time periods.

Effect of (a) pH value, (b) dosage and (c) temperature; (d) Sorption kinetics; (e–f) Pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order equations; (g) Sorption isotherms; (h–i) Langmuir and Freundlich equations.
Fluorescence change and QY of FMIH with different antibiotics.

(a) Fluorescence change and QY of FMIH mixed with different TC concentrations; (b) Fluorescent spectra of FMIH mixed with lower TC concentration and the relationship between F0/F− 1 and concentration.
(a) The viability of NIH/3T3 cells; (b) Fluorescence micrographs.
(a) FTIR analysis; (b) total survey scans of XPS spectra; (c) O1s spectrum; (d) N1s spectrum.
(a) Optical properties; (b) Fluorescence decay; (c–d) Calculated average lifetime.

head.ce:title: Non-toxic fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel based on wood-derived cellulose nanocrystals and carbon dots for efficient sorption and sensitive detection of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For clean, effective, and high value-added use of wood, a novel non-toxic fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel (FMIH) was prepared based on wood-derived cellulose nanocrystals and carbon dots for efficient sorption and sensitive detection of tetracycline (TC). The FMIH’s morphology, chemical structure, optical properties and non-cytotoxicity were investigated. The adsorption performance of FMIH toward TC was studied, indicating that the sorption data were well fitted by the Langmuir and Pseudo-second-order models, and the maximum sorption capacity was 544.4 mg/g. An evaluation of the detection performance of FMIH toward TC showed its excellent selectivity and sensitivity to TC with the detection limit of 0.11 μg/L. Mechanism analysis confirmed that the efficient sorption and sensitive detection was due to specific molecular recognition sites introduced by wood-derived cellulose nanocrystals and carbon dots. Moreover, a cost analysis performed for FMIH and commercial activated carbon (CAC) showed that the cost of removing 1 g of TC using FMIH was almost thirty-four times cheaper than that of CAC. This work could provide a new way for using wood to produce a low-cost, multifunctional, and green adsorbent for removing antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are chemicals that can interfere with the development of living cells and are commonly used in medical treatment and aquaculture (Inyinbor et al., 2018). Humans and animals can only absorb 30% of antibiotics; thus, the majority is excreted. Antibiotics can cause serious public health problems when released into water or soils because of the generation of antimicrobial-resistant bacteria, which has adverse effects on aquatic environments. Hence, antibiotics have become a well-known emerging environmental concern. Various water treatment technologies have been established for removing antibiotics from wastewater, including adsorption (Wang et al., 2022, 2021a), photocatalysis, micro-wave catalysis (Wang et al., 2021b, 2020a), photocatalytic purification (Liu et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2021a; Yue et al., 2021) and advanced oxidation (Lin et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022, 2021b). Adsorption is a practical approach due to its high efficiency and usability. Based on environmental needs and sustainable development strategies, there is an urgent need to design and synthesize green and highly effective adsorbent (Othmani et al., 2021).

Hydrogel has attracted wide attention as an efficient adsorbent for antibiotics because of its excellent adsorption performance (Wang et al., 2019). Minale et al. synthesized manganese dioxide loaded poly(sodium acrylate) hydrogel to remove oxytetracycline (OTC) and showed that the Langmuir isotherm model was the best for illustrating the isotherm data of hydrogel (Minale et al., 2021). Yadav et al. prepared methionine-functionalized graphene oxide/sodium alginate biopolymer nanocomposite hydrogel to deal with moxifloxacin (MOX) and ofloxacin (OFX), determining that the maximum adsorption capacities were 4.11 mg/g for MOX and 3.43 mg/g for OFX (Yadav et al., 2021). Thakur et al. synthesized xanthan gum-cl-poly (itaconic acid)/bentonite hydrogel for adsorptive removal of ampicillin (AMP) and analyzed AMP’s adsorption data, establishing the maximum adsorption capacity (Thakur et al., 2020). Although researchers have made great efforts, improving the adsorption capacity of antibiotics is still a challenge (Melliti et al., 2021). A general solution is to introduce some nanoparticles with excellent properties to further improve the adsorption capacity (Soltani et al., 2018). Based on this consideration, we introduce wood-derived cellulose nanocrystals (WCNs). First, WCNs can provide a skeleton for accelerating the network structure. Second, WCNs can provide many hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups as sorption sites for antibiotics (Wang et al., 2020b, 2017, 2020c; Xu et al., 2019). Therefore, WCNs-based hydrogel has a great potential in developing effective adsorbents for antibiotics.

Tetracycline (TC) is the most widely used among various antibiotics due to its high effectiveness and low-cost. The excessive use of TC has become more severe recently, leading to the presence of TC residue in food such as eggs, meats, milk, and honey (Inyinbor et al., 2018). The intake of TC from food may cause serious health issues, such as affecting teeth health, causing bacterial resistance, and colon disorders. Therefore, there is a crucial need for efficient TC removal. TC is a yellow-colored crystalline compound that has phenolic hydroxyl group (C–OH), dimethyl amino group (–N(CH3)2), acylamino group (–CONH2), and ketoenol conjugated double bond system. Due to this chemical structure, it can be selected as a template molecule for molecular imprinting. Molecular imprinting is an efficient method for obtaining special recognition binding sites to certain template molecules (Zeng et al., 2021). Therefore, molecularly imprinted hydrogel could be used in the selective adsorption of TC.

Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing interest recently (Chen et al., 2021). Many reports have shown that CDs could be applied for fluorescent detection of antibiotics including TC (Yang et al., 2018). Gao et al. fabricated N/S/P co-doped CDs in an alkaline solution at room temperature, which showed ultra-high sensitivity for TC detection (Gao et al., 2020b). Hu et al. synthesized a convenient fluorescence TC sensor based on CDs (Hu et al., 2022). However, the sensor probe application still has some problems; for instance, these CDs tend to agglomerate with the increase of detection and storage time (Zhao et al., 2021). One possible solution for this problem is to develop a stable matrix for embedding CDs, such as a hydrogel.

Based on the above background, we are interested in fabricating a novel fluorescent molecularly imprinted hydrogel (FMIH) based on WCNs and CDs for efficient sorption and sensitive detection of TC. In this FMIH, the WCNs provide a natural skeleton for accelerating the network structure and improving the sorption ability; the CDs act as a fluorescent probe and provide sensitive detection for TC. The morphology, chemical composition, and optical feature of the FMIH were investigated. The sorption performance of the FMIH toward TC removal was examined, in which the effects of pH, dosage, temperature, contact time, initial concentration, and reusability were observed separately. Meanwhile, the detection performance of the FMIH toward TC removal was studied, in which the selectivity and sensitivity was evaluated. The cytotoxicity of FMIH was investigated using 3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay and Live/Dead fluorescence staining method. The sorption and detection mechanism of TC on FMIH was studied. Furthermore, a cost analysis was conducted for FMIH. All these showed that FMIH was a low-cost, multifunctional, and green adsorbent for removing TC.

N,N′-Methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBA, 99%), potassium persulfatee (PPS, 98%), 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidinooxy (TEMPO, 97%), sodium hypochlorite pentahydrate (NaClO.5 H2O, 40%), acrylic acid (AA, 99%), citric acid (CA, 99.5%) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, 98%) were bought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China).

For the synthesis of WCNs, Bleached wood pulp is the starting material for synthesizing WCNs. The bleaching of wood pulp is chemical processing of wood pulp to lighten its color. The bleaching procedures are as follows: wood pulp is exposed to chlorine, washed with a sodium hydroxide solution to remove lignin fragmented by chlorination, treated with sodium hypochlorite, washed with sodium hydroxide again, and given a final treatment with hypochlorite. The main component of the bleached wood pulp is cellulose; other components, such as lignin, pectin, and hemicelluloses, are almost removed. Here 20 g of bleached wood pulp was mixed with water at a mass ratio of 1:50. Subsequently, TEMPO (0.16 g), NaBr (0.8 g), and NaClO (12 mL, 5% chlorine solution) were added and mixed thoroughly. Then, the pH was maintained at 10.0, and the reaction lasted for 4 h. After that, this mixture was washed five times with water and passed through an M110P microfluidizer for six passes. Subsequently, WCNs suspension was prepared.

In the previous work, CDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method (Zhu et al., 2013). First, CA (4.2 g) and ethylenediamine (5.36 mL) were mixed with water (100 mL). This mixture was transferred to a Teflon reaction vessel, sealed, and autoclaved at 473 K for 5 h. The obtained CDs solution was purified using a dialysis bag. Afterward, the CDs were obtained and characterized using TEM (Fig. S1).

The cross-linkage between WCNs and CDs was accomplished using the coupling reaction. Firstly, the CDs solution (2.0 g, 0.6 wt%) was mixed with PBS buffer with a pH value of 7.4 (6.0 mL). Then, EDC (0.03 g) and NHS (0.12 g) were put during stirring. Next, WCNs suspension (12.0 g, 1.0 wt%) was added to the mixture and stirred for a further 12 h. The product was then washed six times with water. A series of WCNs-CDs were obtained by adjusting CDs concentration and optimal WCNs-CDs with the strongest fluorescence intensity were identified (Fig. S2).

WCNs-CDs suspension was suspended in water at a 1:2 mass ratio with vacuum mixing. After that, the prepared WCNs-CDs suspension (1.1 g), AA (1.2 g), MBA (0.03 g), and TC solution (10 g, 0.5 wt%) were thoroughly mixed. Next, PPS (0.045 g) was fully dissolved in an N2 atmosphere. The reaction was kept at 310 K for 4 h. The obtained product was continuously washed with water to remove excess reactants and then eluted using the mixture of SDS (10 wt%) and acetic acid (10 wt%) at a 1:1 vol ratio until no TC was observed using UV–vis spectroscopy. Finally, the hydrogel was cut into small discs, and labeled FMIH for further testing. Fluorescent non-imprinted imprinted hydrogel (FNIH) was synthesized using the same method without the TC solution.

The batch adsorption experiments were conducted in 250 mL conical flasks (Sichuan Shubo Co., Ltd., Sichuan, China), which were put on a magnetic stirrer hotplate with temperature control (Jiangsu Kexi Instrument CO., Ltd., Jiangsu, China). The sorption behaviors (effect of pH, dosage, temperature, contact time and initial concentration, and reusability) of FNIH and FMIH were investigated. The pH value was varied from 2 to 9, the dosage was varied from 0.5 to 3.5 g/L, and the temperature was varied in the range of 288–318 K. The effects of contact time (5–300 min) were tested. The concentration was varied from 5 to 150 mg/L. All these experiments were averaged repeated three times.

Scanning electron microscopy image was taken using JEOL 7000-Ffield-emission scanning electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were collected in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR)-FTIR mode via the iS50 FTIR Spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). Fluorescence spectrum was obtained via F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–visible spectrum was obtained via Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured using Sigma Probe spectrometer (VG Scientific, UK).

The novel FMIH preparation route is illustrated in Fig. 1. WCNs, CDs, AA, and MBA are essential raw materials for the FMIH. The WCNs provided a natural skeleton for accelerating the network structure and then improved the sorption ability; the CDs acted as a fluorescent probe and provided sensitive detection for TC. Given the molecular imprinting technology and economic feasibility requirement (Kadhem et al., 2021), the more common monomer and crosslinker, AA and MBA, were used. Firstly, WCNs-CDs were prepared via covalent bonding between WCNs and CDs during the synthesis process. Secondly, WCNs-CDs were thoroughly mixed with AA, MBA, and TC. After that, the PPS initiator was added, and then AA monomer and MBA crosslinker formed chemical bonds with WCNs to build hydrogel. Finally, TC was eluted, and the FMIH was prepared.

Fig. 2a illustrates the FMIH microstructures. FMIH had a 3D porous structure. A 3D porous structure was constructed using the radical polymerization, and CDs were wrapped. Pore characteristics of FMIH were evaluated in Fig. S3, and the specific surface area and average pore diameter were 12.4741 m2/g and 5.5218 nm, respectively. Moreover, an 18.5° contact angle showed the hydrophilicity of FMIH (Han et al., 2020).

Fig. 2b illustrates XRD patterns of WCNs, WCNs-CDs, and FMIH. WCNs exhibited three peaks at 14.8°, 16.4°, and 22.5° associated with (101), (
10
1
¯
), and (002) planes, respectively, indicating the crystallographic form of cellulose I (Sun et al., 2015). These three peaks could be found in WCNs-CDs, indicating that the grafting of CDs did not change the crystalline structure of WCNs. In the pattern of FMIH, these peaks cannot be found clearly, which was attributed to the destruction of short-range order due to crosslinking.

Fig. 2c illustrates the FTIR spectra of WCNs, WCNs-CDs, and FMIH. The 3338 cm−1 band was from O–H stretching vibration of WCNs, but for WCNs-CDs and FMIH, this band was also related to the mutual O–H…N–H hydrogen bonding (Singha et al., 2019). Additionally, a significant characteristic band (1053 cm−1) closely related to cellulose species was found in both WCNs and WCNs-CDs (Yuan et al., 2021). Absorption appearing at 1653 cm–1 was designated as amide I peak of secondary amides, which confirmed that CDs were grafted onto the WCNs (Mahapatra et al., 2020). Moreover, the FTIR spectrum of FMIH exhibited several bands different from those of WCNs and WCNs-CDs. Specifically, the 1695 cm−1 peak originated from CO stretching vibration of amide and carboxylic acid confirming that the WCNs-CDs underwent the polymerization reaction with the help of AA and MBA (Mitra et al., 2020).

To gain further insights into the chemical structure of FMIH, XPS was employed. In the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2d), four bands observed in FMIH at 533, 400, and 285 eV were associated with O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. Fig. 2e shows the high-resolution O1s spectrum. The O1s region was split into three components associated with –O–H (533.6 eV), –COO– (532.2 eV) and –CO (531.6 eV) (Mitra et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2017, 2016). For the N1s region (Fig. 2f), the peaks at 401.9, 399.8, and 398.7 eV appeared, which were related to protonated amine (–NH3 +), amine (–NH2), and imine (=N–) groups (Kwak et al., 2020).

The optical properties of FMIH were investigated. The UV–vis spectrum exhibited a peak at about 356 nm (Fig. 2g), which could have originated from the n–π * transition of aromatic systems with carbon-nitrogen and carbon-oxygen double bonds in the CDs as well as the space conjugations of –CONH–, –OH, –CON= , and –COOH/–COO – functional groups in FMIH (Cheng et al., 2019; Dutta et al., 2021a, 2021b; Mitra et al., 2020). The maximum emission peak of FMIH was centered at 446 nm under the 368 nm excitation (Fig. 2h). When λex moved from 323 to 413 nm, the λem of FMIH had a bathochromic shift from 436 to 510 nm. The emissions of FMIH with excitation-dependent characteristics were further confirmed.

Moreover, FMIH exhibited apparent blue fluorescence and had 4.8% quantum yield (QY). The photo-stability of FMIH was studied for different time periods (Fig. 2i). The fluorescence and QY were almost unchanged after one week. Hence, FMIH had the potential for a fluorescent probe.

The pH value is a crucial parameter for TC sorption. Hence, the sorption process is investigated at a pH of 2–9 ( Fig. 3a). Three sections were designated according to the curve trend of adsorption capacity (Q e) with pH value. As the pH was 2 and 3, the Q e of FMIH was high. According to the zero potential of FMIH (Fig. S4), FMIH carried a negative charge in this first section (Zhao et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the TC existed positive charge, and the cationic nature of TC was correlated with the possible protonation of dialkyl amine functional groups (Sun et al., 2016). Hence, there was a very strong electrostatic attraction. The second section was in the pH scope of 4–7, the Q e of FMIH began to decline. This hydrogel possessed only weak electrostatic attraction with neutral TC. When the pH entered the third section (pH > 8), the Q e of the FMIH continued to decline. The TC existed negative charge, and this hydrogel possessed electrostatic repulsion with TC (Gao et al., 2020a). Consequently, three was chosen to be the optimal pH value.

The effect of FMIH dosage on TC sorption was also studied (Fig. 3b). The removal efficiency increased with a dosage increase from 0.5 to 2.0 g/L; it increased slightly with the dosage from 2.0 to 3.5 g/L. This trend may be explained by more sorption sites being obtained at higher FMIH dosage, leading to higher removal efficiency. Once the FMIH dosage was high enough, TC transfer to the FMIH surface seemed to be the rate-controlling step in the reduction. Since the removal efficiency of FMIH was not growing fast with the dosage from 2.0 to 3.5 g/L, one moderate dosage, 3.0 g/L, was used for further experiments.

As a major parameter during the adsorption process, the effects of temperature on the adsorption process of FMIH toward TC were explored (Fig. 3c). The Q e of FMIH was observed to rise, showing that high temperature was favorable to TC sorption and this sorption process could be endothermic. This change is expected because higher temperature increases the kinetic energy of TC; thus, the increased motion of the TC molecules causes them to diffuse faster. Meanwhile, it was observed that the Q e of FMIH in 298 K reached 83% than that in 318 K. Hence, to save energy and reduce cost, 298 K was used subsequently.

TC adsorption as a function of contact time was further observed (Fig. 3d). For FMIH, the Q t increased extremely rapidly in the first 50 min. Owing to the occupation of sorption sites, the Q t of FMIH decreased slowly. Compared with FNIH, the Q t of FMIH was high within the same contact time. The common equations were applied for fitting sorption kinetic data of FMIH, including pseudo-first order equation and pseudo-second order equation. Fitting results and corresponding kinetic parameters are plotted in Fig. 3e and f. The kinetic data were better fitted using pseudo-second order equation with a higher correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9915, showing the TC sorption onto FMIH was based on the chemisorption and a rate-limiting process (Zhou et al., 2016).

The initial concentration can impact adsorption. As shown in Fig. 3g, the Q e of FMIH and FNIH increased significantly with increasing concentration. The common models could be applied in analyzing sorption isotherms, including the Langmuir and Freundlich equations. Fitting results are plotted in Fig. 3h and i. The experimental data were better fitted using the Langmuir equation with a higher R2 of 0.9952, showing this sorption on the FNIH was monolayer (Zhou et al., 2017). The maximum adsorption capacity (Q m) of 544.4 mg/g was also obtained. Some researchers synthesized other related adsorbents for TC, most of which showed lower Q m, as listed in Table 1.

Moreover, reusability is an essential criterion for considering a material applicable for practical purposes. Hence, the adsorption–desorption cycles were taken using the eluent of the mixture of SDS (10 wt%) and acetic acid (10 wt%) at a 1:1 vol ratio; the results are shown in Fig. S5. It was observed that the FMIH still kept 85.0% of the initial Q e after six cycles. Therefore, the FMIH could be reused and regenerated.

To investigate the sorption selectivity of FMIH, structure analog OTC and different structural CIP were used as references with an initial concentration of 150 mg/L. Table S1 shows the adsorption capacities, distribution and selectivity coefficients of FMIH and FNIH. The sorption capacity of FMIH for TC was higher than that of OTC and CIP. Moreover, TC’s distribution coefficient (K d) of TC was larger than that of OTC and CIP. This led to the formation of many specific sorption sites for TC in FMIH, which could improve the adsorption of TC (Sun et al., 2016). The selectivity coefficient (β) of FMIH for OTC was larger than that of CIP, which was attributed to the structure of OTC being similar to that of TC. All these showed that FMIH exhibited high sorption selectivity toward TC.

To study the selectivity, fluorescence change and QY of FMIH were examined when this probe was challenged with eight antibiotics (CIP, ENR, OFX, SMZ, TC, SD, CAP, and OTC). Fig. 4a and b demonstrated that the TC could cause significant fluorescence quenching compared with the other seven antibiotics. This may be explained by the TC having stronger hydrogen-bond interactions with CDs; thus, TC could absorb more photons decreasing the number of photons available to work on the CDs and causing the strongest fluorescence quenching signal (Gao et al., 2020b). These results display the excellent selectivity of FMIH toward TC.

Different concentrations of TC were thoroughly mixed with the FMIH to study its selectivity. Fig. 5a shows the change of fluorescence quenching with TC concentration. There were linear correlations between the QY and TC concentrations from 1 to 200 mg/L. Moreover, the fluorescence emission spectrum of FMIH mixing with a lower concentration of TC solution was collected in Fig. 5b. As expected, the signature of the peak at 446 nm was observed to decrease in intensity. The inset plot clearly showed the linear relation between the intensity ratio (F0/F)− 1 and TC concentrations (0.2–1.0 μg/L) with the detection limit of 0.11 μg/L. The linear range and detection limit were compared with the other reports (Table S2). It was observed that the FMIH had better detection sensitivity.

TC detection in tap and lake water was performed by spiking different known concentrations of TC to demonstrate the applicability of FMIH. As displayed in Table S3, the average TC recoveries are range from 99.81% to 102.27%, with relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 3% from three independent experiments. The results suggested that FMIH had great potential for practical applications.

The cytotoxicity of FMIH was investigated in NIH 3T3 cells using MTT assay and the Live/Dead fluorescence staining method. As presented in Fig. 6a, the cell viability of FMIH and FNIH was around 90% as the concentration was in the range of 15–960 μg/mL, indicating that FMIH was non-toxic. As live cells were blue (stained by H33342) and dead cells were red (stained by PI) fluorescence, most NIH 3T3 cells entrapped in FMIH were alive, which was evidenced by the various blue dots throughout the image in Fig. 6b. Therefore, FMIH can be used as a non-toxic adsorbent.

The FMIH after adsorption processes were analyzed using the FTIR to understand the sorption mechanism ( Fig. 7a). The 3338 cm−1 band due to the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations was found to have obvious change. Furthermore, the 1448 cm−1 band due to C–N stretching vibration increased. It was indicated that the TC sorption may be closely related to hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups.

The FMIH’s chemical environment after TC adsorption was further analyzed using the XPS. Fig. 7b shows the total survey scans. The peaks at 285, 400 and 533 eV appeared which were related to C1s, N1s, and O1s orbits, respectively. Fig. 7c illustrates the high-resolution O1s spectrum. The O1s band can be divided into three components associated with –CO, –COO– and –O–H (Mitra et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2017, 2016). These component peaks increased marginally to the higher binding energy after sorption. Moreover, no other component could be found, confirming that TC interacted with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups via non covalent bonds. Based on the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 7d), the peaks at 401.9, 399.8, and 398.7 eV appeared, which were related to protonated amine (–NH3 +), imine (=N–), and amine (–NH2) groups. The intensity of the signature from –NH3 + was observed to rise after TC sorption, indicating that the amino group participated in the TC sorption.

UV–vis and fluorescence excitation spectra were obtained to determine the detection mechanism. The TC’s UV–vis band had an overlap area with that of FMIH ( Fig. 8a), indicating that TC detection could be due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or internal filter effect (IFE). As shown in Fig. 8b–d, the average fluorescence life of FMIH (τ *= 5.03 ns) was very close to that of FMIH mixed with TC (τ *= 4.91 ns), confirming that there was no FRET. Therefore, the IFE was the primary reason.

According to the above-mentioned analytical result, the TC sorption and detection mechanism of FMIH can be conducted as follows ( Fig. 9). Firstly, TC was enriched into the interface of FMIH via electrostatic interaction. After that, TC was gradually diffused to the inside of FMIH along 3D porous structures. Some TC interacted with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in this diffusion process. Due to these specific structures, FMIH exhibited strong TC adsorption ability. TC was enriched into the CDs simultaneously, leading to the fluorescence quenching of FMIH. Therefore, FMIH owned the target detection capability.

The net cost of producing an adsorbent usually includes the raw materials, reaction reagents, and the cost of operating reaction equipment (Gupta et al., 2018). Table S4 shows the total cost of preparing 1 kg of FMIH. Compared to commercially activated carbon (CAC), FMIH was almost three times cheaper than CAC. Moreover, Table S5 shows the cost incurred in adsorbing 1 g TC using FMIH compared to CAC. From this cost evaluation (Fan et al., 2016), the cost of FMIH for removing 1 g of TC was only CNY 0.17, which was almost thirty-four times cheaper than that of CAC (CNY 5.74). This confirmed that FMIH was the economically viable option for TC adsorption.

A non-toxic FMIH based on WCNs and CDs was prepared with effective sorption and sensitive detection abilities for TC. The WCNs provided a skeleton for accelerating the network structure and improved the sorption ability for TC. Moreover, CDs acted as fluorescent probes for TC and provided adsorption sites. The FMIH possessed efficient adsorption properties with a maximum adsorption capacity of 544.4 mg/g. Meanwhile, the FMIH was employed for selective and quantitative detection of TC. This hydrogel retained its effective adsorption ability even after six repeated regenerations. Moreover, using cost analysis, the economic feasibility of FMIH over activated carbon was confirmed. This study supplied a new way to produce low-cost adsorbents with effective sorption and sensitive detection abilities for antibiotics.

Qiuyan Luo: Investigation. Shan He: Software. Yifeng Huang: Software. Zihua Lei: Investigation. Jianzheng Qiao: Software. Qing Li: Investigation. Dong Xu: Software. Xin Guo: Conceptualization. Writing – original draft, Validation. Yiqiang Wu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by The Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 2021RC4062), Hunan Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Project, China (Grant No. 2020RC3043), and Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. CX20210880).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2022.114528.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
